Regulation Status: Afghanistan has no AI legislation, policy, or institutional engagement with AI governance as of March 2026.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any kind address AI development or deployment.
Governance Type: No governance structures exist; the Taliban government has not engaged with AI policy.
Actor Involvement: No participation in any international AI governance forum or regional initiative.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist.

Regulation Status: Albania has no national AI legislation. As an EU candidate country it is expected to align with the AI Act, and has signed the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (2024).
Policy Lever: No binding AI policy instruments. Regulatory approach relies on future EU acquis adoption and CoE Convention alignment.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance body; responsibility falls under general ICT and data protection institutions.
Actor Involvement: Engages through EU accession process and Council of Europe AI Convention. Regional Western Balkan digital cooperation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms.
Specific Laws: Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (signed 2024)

Regulation Status: Algeria has no dedicated national AI legislation as of April 2026. The country relies on its participation in the African Union's Continental AI Strategy (approved July 2024) and a national digitalization roadmap that mentions AI without binding regulatory provisions.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms consist primarily of alignment with the AU Continental AI Strategy framework and references within the national digitalization roadmap. No AI-specific regulatory instruments or sectoral guidelines have been formally enacted.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Post and Telecommunications coordinates digital policy without a dedicated AI governance unit or inter-ministerial AI oversight body. Governance remains integrated within general digitalization portfolios rather than specialized.
Actor Involvement: Algeria participates in AU Continental AI Strategy discussions and maintains observer status in some OECD digital economy working groups. Regional and international engagement is limited to non-binding strategic forums.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI governance, as binding national legislation and dedicated oversight bodies have not been established.

Regulation Status: Andorra has no dedicated AI legislation as of April 2026. The country remains aligned with Council of Europe AI frameworks and indirectly follows EU developments through its customs union relationship, with governance primarily dependent on general data protection provisions.
Policy Lever: Andorra relies on its data protection law (Law 15/2003 on Personal Data Protection) as an indirect governance mechanism for some AI applications. No AI-specific policy instruments or sectoral regulations have been enacted.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance body exists in Andorra. The Andorran Data Protection Agency (Autoritat de Protecció de Dades) handles data-related aspects with limited indirect authority over AI systems.
Actor Involvement: Andorra participates in Council of Europe AI governance frameworks and monitors EU regulatory developments. Domestic AI industry involvement is minimal due to the small economy and limited tech sector presence.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with only indirect oversight through data protection compliance; no AI-specific enforcement infrastructure exists.
Specific Laws: Law 15/2003 on Personal Data Protection

Regulation Status: Angola has no AI-specific legislation, national AI strategy, or dedicated regulatory framework as of April 2026. The country relies on general ICT and data protection laws that do not explicitly address artificial intelligence governance.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments exist. General digital and telecommunications policies under the Ministry of Telecommunications do not contain targeted AI governance mechanisms.
Governance Type: Angola has no dedicated AI governance body or institutional structure. Digital policy oversight remains with the Ministry of Telecommunications, which lacks AI-specific mandates or capacity.
Actor Involvement: Angola participates in African Union Continental AI Strategy discussions but has not formalized bilateral AI governance agreements or regional AI cooperation frameworks. Domestic institutional engagement with AI policy remains minimal.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms, oversight bodies, or compliance frameworks exist in Angola.

Regulation Status: Antigua and Barbuda has no AI-specific legislation or regulatory framework as of April 2026. The country remains in a pre-regulatory phase with no domestic AI governance mechanisms established despite signing the Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI in 2023.
Policy Lever: No dedicated AI policy mechanisms exist. The country relies on general data protection and ICT frameworks that are underdeveloped and not specifically tailored to AI governance.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structure has been established. General telecommunications and digital policy matters are handled by the telecommunications authority without specialized AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Antigua and Barbuda is engaged through CARICOM coordination, having signed the Santiago Declaration (2023) on ethical AI principles. Regional participation reflects commitment to international norms but without domestic implementation structures.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as there are no AI-specific laws or policies to enforce.

Regulation Status: Argentina has multiple AI bills in congress including a Bill on Personal Data Protection in AI Systems. A national AI strategy exists but no comprehensive binding legislation has been enacted.
Policy Lever: Soft policy through ethical guidelines and national AI plan. Proposed legislation would regulate personal data use in AI training. Provincial AI protocols emerging.
Governance Type: The Secretariat of Science and Technology coordinates national AI policy. No dedicated AI regulatory body yet established.
Actor Involvement: Active in Latin American AI governance through Santiago Declaration, Cartagena Declaration, and OECD AI policy engagement. Bilateral cooperation with EU on digital standards.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement. Existing data protection authority (AAIP) may gain expanded mandate under proposed legislation.
Specific Laws: National AI Plan (2019)|Draft Bill on Personal Data Protection in AI Systems

Regulation Status: Armenia has no national AI legislation as of April 2026. The country relies on the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI as its primary international commitment but has not yet translated this into domestic regulatory frameworks.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments exist. Indirect governance occurs through general data protection laws and ICT frameworks that predate AI considerations.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance body exists. The Innovation and Technology Agency provides minimal coordination on AI-related matters within a broader technology remit.
Actor Involvement: Armenia participates in the Council of Europe AI Convention and Eastern Partnership digital cooperation initiatives but lacks domestic institutional capacity for AI governance. International engagement remains limited to multilateral frameworks.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms exist; oversight is limited to general data protection authorities with minimal AI expertise.

Regulation Status: Australia paused mandatory AI guardrails in favor of a standards-led approach. The National AI Plan 2025 and voluntary AI6 practices guide governance. An AI Safety Institute (AU$29.9M) became operational in early 2026. Privacy Act reforms introduce mandatory automated decision-making transparency effective December 2026.
Policy Lever: Voluntary AI6 guardrails, standards-based approach, and sector-specific enforcement through eSafety Commissioner, OAIC, and ACCC. Privacy Act reforms add binding transparency requirements for automated decisions.
Governance Type: No single AI regulator. eSafety Commissioner is the most active enforcer. OAIC handles privacy. AI Safety Institute provides research and evaluation. Department of Industry coordinates national strategy.
Actor Involvement: Active in OECD, GPAI, Quad AI Partnership, AUKUS technology cooperation, and International Network of AI Safety Institutes. Hosts a national AI Safety Institute.
Enforcement Level: eSafety Commissioner issued legal notices to four AI chatbot companies (Oct 2025) with penalties up to AU$49.5M and forced withdrawal of three nudify services. OAIC investigates AI privacy breaches.
Specific Laws: Privacy Act 1988 (amended 2024)|Online Safety Act 2021|National AI Plan 2025

Regulation Status: Austria implements the EU AI Act with phased obligations. National implementation is underway but Austria has not yet been among the first to designate all competent authorities.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act risk-based framework combined with GDPR enforcement and sectoral guidelines. No significant supplementary national legislation beyond EU requirements.
Governance Type: Austrian Data Protection Authority (DSB) is the expected lead competent authority, coordinating with federal ministries. Formal designation still being finalized.
Actor Involvement: Participates in EU AI Board, OECD AI Policy Observatory, and G7 Hiroshima AI Process follow-up.
Enforcement Level: DSB has enforcement powers under the EU AI Act framework but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken yet across the entire EU.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)|Austrian Data Protection Act

Regulation Status: Azerbaijan approved an AI Strategy 2025-2028 by presidential decree in March 2025 but has not enacted binding AI legislation.
Policy Lever: Strategy-based policy approach focusing on AI capacity building and digital economy development rather than regulatory mandates.
Governance Type: Presidential administration coordinates AI strategy with the Ministry of Digital Development and Transport. No dedicated AI regulatory body.
Actor Involvement: Engages through Council of Europe and Turkic States cooperation frameworks. Bilateral partnerships with Turkey and Israel on AI development.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms.
Specific Laws: AI Strategy 2025-2028 (March 2025)

Regulation Status: The Bahamas has no AI-specific legislation, policy framework, or national AI strategy as of April 2026. The country relies on general data protection principles but does not address AI governance distinctly.
Policy Lever: No dedicated AI policy instruments exist. General data protection frameworks do not specifically address AI systems, algorithmic accountability, or AI-related risks.
Governance Type: No formal AI governance structure, regulatory body, or institutional framework for AI oversight has been established. Governance remains fragmented across general regulatory authorities.
Actor Involvement: The Bahamas participates in CARICOM regional AI discussions and the Santiago Declaration on AI in the Americas but has not translated international engagement into domestic policy action. Limited coordination between government agencies on AI matters.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms specific to AI regulation exist; AI activities are not monitored or subject to compliance requirements.

Regulation Status: Bahrain's Shura Council unanimously approved a standalone AI Regulation Law (38 articles) in April 2024, covering risk classification and specific prohibitions. It awaits lower house approval.
Policy Lever: The draft law includes prohibitions on AI systems that could incite societal unrest, with criminal penalties and extended organizational liability. Biometric and discrimination provisions included.
Governance Type: Bahrain's Information and eGovernment Authority coordinates AI policy. The draft law would establish formal oversight mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Active in GCC AI governance discussions. Bahrain is the most advanced Gulf state in formal AI legislation development.
Enforcement Level: Draft law includes criminal penalties but enforcement will depend on final enactment and implementing regulations.
Specific Laws: Draft AI Regulation Law (Shura Council approved April 2024)

Regulation Status: Bangladesh remains in the draft legislation phase with the National AI Policy v3.0 (2024) under development but not yet enacted into law. No binding AI-specific legislation exists, though the government has signaled intent to formalize AI governance through sectoral guidelines in healthcare, agriculture, and climate adaptation.
Policy Lever: Bangladesh relies on soft policy instruments including the draft National AI Policy and voluntary sectoral guidelines rather than binding legal frameworks. International cooperation through UN and South Asian forums provides some policy direction, but enforcement mechanisms remain absent.
Governance Type: The ICT Division under the Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Information Technology coordinates AI policy development without a dedicated standalone AI governance body. Interagency coordination exists informally but lacks institutional formalization.
Actor Involvement: Bangladesh participates selectively in UN digital initiatives and South Asian regional cooperation forums (SAARC, BIMSTEC) but maintains limited bilateral AI engagement with major tech powers. Domestic stakeholder involvement appears concentrated in government and limited private sector consultation.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist as the draft policy remains unenacted, with absent regulatory bodies, audit mechanisms, or penalty provisions for non-compliance.

Regulation Status: Barbados has no AI-specific legislation or dedicated regulatory framework as of April 2026. The country relies on general data protection and consumer protection laws that do not explicitly address AI systems. Regulatory development remains limited to regional discussions through CARICOM.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments or mechanisms exist in Barbados. Existing general regulatory frameworks do not contain provisions targeting AI development or deployment.
Governance Type: Barbados has no dedicated AI governance structure or authority. Digital policy matters are handled by general regulatory bodies without specialized AI oversight capacity.
Actor Involvement: Barbados engages through regional mechanisms including CARICOM digital governance discussions and the Santiago Declaration (2023). Domestic AI policy development remains minimal with limited private sector or civil society involvement in AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms or oversight bodies exist to monitor or enforce AI-related compliance.

Regulation Status: Belarus has no AI-specific legislation as of April 2026. The country relies on general IT sector frameworks and has not developed dedicated AI regulatory provisions or national AI strategy.
Policy Lever: Belarus uses indirect incentives through the High-Tech Park regime to encourage technology sector development. No AI-specific policy instruments or regulatory mechanisms exist.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance body exists in Belarus. The High-Tech Park authority manages general technology sector development but lacks AI-specific oversight responsibilities.
Actor Involvement: Belarus has minimal participation in international AI governance frameworks due to political isolation and limited multilateral engagement. Domestic AI policy development is not coordinated with international standards or agreements.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as Belarus has not established AI-specific legal or regulatory frameworks.

Regulation Status: Belgium implements the EU AI Act. The Belgian Data Protection Authority (APD) leads implementation and has been active on algorithmic decision-making cases.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act risk-based framework combined with strong GDPR enforcement. Belgium's institutional location as EU capital gives particular visibility to implementation efforts.
Governance Type: APD leads AI Act implementation, coordinating with federal and regional authorities across Belgium's complex institutional structure.
Actor Involvement: Active in EU AI Board. Belgium's role as host of EU institutions gives it significant influence in implementation discussions.
Enforcement Level: APD has established enforcement infrastructure and has issued decisions on algorithmic processing. No AI Act-specific enforcement yet (none issued EU-wide).
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)

Regulation Status: Belize has not enacted AI-specific legislation or developed a national AI policy framework as of May 2026. The country operates without formal regulatory structures governing artificial intelligence technologies, relying instead on general ICT and data protection frameworks that predate AI-focused considerations.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidelines, or regulatory mechanisms are currently in place. Belize relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks that lack explicit AI governance components.
Governance Type: Belize has not established dedicated AI governance structures, interagency committees, or designated regulatory bodies for AI oversight. AI governance responsibilities remain fragmented across general technology and telecommunications authorities without coordinated frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Belize maintains minimal participation in international AI governance initiatives and multilateral AI policy discussions. Engagement with organizations such as the OECD, UNESCO, or UN AI processes remains limited, and domestic multi-stakeholder processes for AI policy development have not been established.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms exist; general ICT and data protection frameworks lack dedicated enforcement structures for AI-related compliance.

Regulation Status: Benin has published a national AI strategy aligned with the African Union Continental AI Strategy but lacks binding legislative framework for AI regulation. The approach remains strategy-based and advisory, with no domestic legal enforcement mechanisms specific to AI.
Policy Lever: Benin primarily relies on capacity building initiatives and strategic guidelines rather than regulatory mandates. Policy mechanisms are centered on national strategy implementation and participation in continental frameworks (AU and Smart Africa) without formal legal instruments.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Digital Economy coordinates AI policy development, but no dedicated AI regulatory body or agency exists. Governance remains decentralized across sector ministries without specialized oversight structure.
Actor Involvement: Benin actively participates in the African Union Continental AI Strategy and Smart Africa Alliance frameworks at continental and regional levels. Engagement includes government and regional institutions but limited bilateral AI governance partnerships or domestic multi-stakeholder consultation structures.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanism exists; AI obligations are non-binding and compliance is entirely voluntary with no sanctioning authority or framework.

Regulation Status: Bhutan is in early-stage AI engagement, developing a principles-based approach aligned with Gross National Happiness (GNH) rather than enacting binding legislation. No formal AI-specific laws have been enacted as of May 2026, with policy remaining centered on voluntary guidelines and strategic frameworks.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms consist primarily of GNH-aligned AI principles and draft strategy documents operating as voluntary guidance. The approach relies on philosophical frameworks and strategic direction from the GNH Commission rather than enforceable regulatory tools or sectoral mandates.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Information and Communications leads AI policy development with the GNH Commission providing philosophical and ethical oversight, creating a dual-institutional structure. Coordination between these entities appears informal rather than through established formal mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Bhutan engages with international organizations including UNDP and UNESCO on AI readiness assessments and capacity building, with limited bilateral AI governance partnerships. Domestic stakeholder engagement remains limited, reflecting early-stage development, though international consultation channels are active.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanism exists; obligations are not tied to sanctioning authority and compliance with voluntary guidelines is effectively optional.

Regulation Status: Bolivia has no dedicated AI legislation as of April 2026. The country relies on regional frameworks including the Santiago Declaration (2023) and Cartagena Declaration (2024) for AI governance guidance, with no domestic legal framework specifically addressing artificial intelligence.
Policy Lever: Bolivia lacks AI-specific policy instruments. General digitalization policies may reference AI aspirationally, but no binding policy mechanisms or regulatory tools targeting AI development, deployment, or governance have been implemented.
Governance Type: Bolivia has no dedicated AI governance body. Digital policy coordination remains under the Ministry of Communications, with no specialized institutional structure for AI oversight or regulation.
Actor Involvement: Bolivia participates in regional Latin American AI governance frameworks, primarily through the Santiago Declaration (2023) and Cartagena Declaration (2024). Engagement is limited to regional coordination with minimal active domestic AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as Bolivia has not established dedicated AI laws or oversight bodies capable of monitoring or penalizing non-compliance.

Regulation Status: Bosnia and Herzegovina has no dedicated AI legislation or national AI strategy as of April 2026. The country's governance structure remains fragmented between the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina and Republika Srpska, creating significant barriers to unified AI policy development.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments exist. The complex multi-entity governance structure prevents coordinated national-level AI regulation.
Governance Type: No centralized AI governance body has been established. Institutional fragmentation between entities continues to impede coordinated AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Bosnia and Herzegovina maintains Council of Europe participation and holds EU candidate status, which may drive future alignment with EU AI Act standards. Limited international engagement on AI policy coordination exists at present.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms or oversight bodies are operational.

Regulation Status: Botswana has no AI-specific legislation as of April 2026. The country lacks a dedicated national AI strategy and relies on general ICT and digital transformation frameworks without AI-focused regulatory provisions.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments exist. General ICT policy through the Ministry of Communications references digital transformation but contains no AI-targeted mechanisms or guidelines.
Governance Type: Botswana has no dedicated AI governance body. The Ministry of Communications handles general digital policy coordination without specialized AI oversight structures.
Actor Involvement: Botswana participates in African Union digital economy discussions and the Smart Africa Alliance, with limited bilateral AI governance engagement at the continental level.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms or oversight bodies exist to monitor or enforce AI-related compliance.

Regulation Status: Brazil's AI Bill 2338/2023 was approved by the Senate (Dec 2024) and forwarded to the Chamber of Deputies (March 2025), where a special committee was formed. Not yet enacted. The bill adopts an EU-style risk-based framework with strict liability provisions. ANPD launched a pilot AI regulatory sandbox in June 2025.
Policy Lever: Draft legislation includes risk classification, prohibited systems, and strict liability. ANPD actively applies data protection law (LGPD) to AI contexts. Copyright in AI training is a major debate.
Governance Type: ANPD (data protection authority) would become the central AI governance authority under the draft bill. Multiple agencies coordinate on elections (TSE), consumer protection (Senacon), and competition (CADE).
Actor Involvement: Active in G20 AI governance (hosted 2023 presidency), Santiago/Cartagena Declarations, and OECD AI policy discussions. Brazil shaped the LAC regional AI governance approach.
Enforcement Level: ANPD enforces LGPD against AI practices (Meta enforcement actions). Election authority (TSE) issued binding AI rules for 2024 elections. Formal AI Act enforcement pending enactment.
Specific Laws: Bill 2338/2023 (Senate approved Dec 2024)|LGPD (2018)|TSE AI Electoral Rules (2024)

Regulation Status: Brunei maintains a light-touch regulatory approach to AI, relying on the voluntary AI Governance and Ethics Guide adopted through ASEAN alignment in 2025. No binding national AI legislation has been enacted, reflecting a preference for enabling AI adoption over restrictive regulation.
Policy Lever: Primary policy mechanisms include the voluntary ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics and the Digital Economy Masterplan 2025, both designed to facilitate rather than constrain AI development. These non-binding frameworks guide industry practice without legal mandates.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Transport and Infocommunications coordinates digital policy including AI matters, but no dedicated AI regulatory body or agency has been established. Governance remains distributed across existing ministries without centralized AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Brunei participates in ASEAN-level AI governance initiatives through the ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics framework adopted in February 2024. Bilateral AI partnerships remain limited, with engagement primarily at the regional ASEAN level.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is minimal given the voluntary nature of existing guidelines; no dedicated enforcement mechanisms, penalties, or compliance monitoring structures are documented.

Regulation Status: Bulgaria implements the EU AI Act but has been slower to designate national competent authorities. The Commission for Personal Data Protection (CPDP) is expected to lead implementation.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act risk-based framework applies. Limited additional national policy instruments beyond EU requirements.
Governance Type: CPDP is the expected lead authority. Institutional capacity for AI-specific oversight is developing.
Actor Involvement: Participates in EU AI Board. Limited proactive engagement compared to western EU states.
Enforcement Level: CPDP has general enforcement authority under EU frameworks. AI-specific enforcement capacity is limited.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)

Regulation Status: Burkina Faso has no AI-specific legislation or governance framework as of April 2026. The country's political instability and military administration have not prioritized AI policy development, leaving the sector entirely unregulated.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments, frameworks, or strategic initiatives are currently in place. The government has not adopted any specific mechanisms to govern AI development or deployment.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structures exist. Political instability and institutional constraints continue to impede the development of regulatory bodies or oversight mechanisms for AI.
Actor Involvement: Burkina Faso maintains only nominal participation in African Union AI frameworks with no active engagement in AI policy development. Domestic stakeholders have minimal involvement in AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as the country lacks both legal frameworks and institutional capacity to monitor or enforce AI-related rules.

Regulation Status: Burundi has no AI-specific legislation, national AI strategy, or regulatory framework as of April 2026. The country lacks dedicated policy instruments or governance structures for artificial intelligence oversight.
Policy Lever: No AI policy mechanisms, regulatory instruments, or voluntary guidelines exist. AI governance is not addressed in any formal policy documents.
Governance Type: No AI governance structures or institutions have been established. Responsibility for AI oversight, if any exists informally, is unclear and uncoordinated.
Actor Involvement: Burundi has minimal involvement in international AI governance frameworks, multilateral AI initiatives, or regional digital policy coordination. Limited institutional capacity for AI engagement.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, regulatory bodies, or oversight capacity for AI-related activities exist.

Regulation Status: Cambodia has no binding AI legislation as of April 2026. The draft national AI strategy launched for public consultation in June 2025 remains in consultation phase without formal adoption or legislative framework. Governance currently relies on ASEAN frameworks and indirect regulation through existing data protection laws.
Policy Lever: Primary policy mechanisms include the Personal Data Protection Law (2023) which indirectly governs AI data practices, and the draft national AI strategy under consultation. No dedicated AI-specific legislation or binding regulatory instruments have been enacted.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications coordinates digital policy without a dedicated AI governance body. Governance relies on ASEAN frameworks and informal coordination mechanisms rather than a dedicated institutional structure for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Cambodia engages through ASEAN AI governance frameworks and maintains bilateral partnerships with Japan and South Korea on digital development. International actor involvement is growing but remains limited in scope compared to comprehensive domestic governance.
Enforcement Level: No active enforcement mechanism exists for AI governance, as binding legislation has not been enacted and the draft strategy remains in consultation phase.
Specific Laws: Personal Data Protection Law (2023)

Regulation Status: Cameroon has no AI-specific legislation, national AI strategy, or dedicated regulatory framework as of May 2026. AI governance remains absent at the national level, with only indirect engagement through African Union continental initiatives and no explicit mention of AI in domestic policy documents.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments exist in Cameroon. General digital policy is handled through the Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications without targeted AI regulations, guidelines, standards, or dedicated funding mechanisms.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance body exists in Cameroon. The Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications manages broader digital policy but lacks an AI-specific institutional mandate, oversight structure, or formal coordination mechanisms with sectoral regulators.
Actor Involvement: Cameroon's only formal engagement on AI occurs through African Union Continental AI Strategy discussions as a member state. No domestic stakeholder coordination mechanisms, industry working groups, civil society consultation processes, or bilateral AI cooperation agreements are evident.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI governance in Cameroon, as there are no binding AI regulations or obligations to enforce.

Regulation Status: AIDA died when Parliament was prorogued January 6, 2025. The Carney government declared AIDA 'off the table as drafted' and created a Ministry of AI and Digital Innovation. A Canadian AI Safety Institute launched November 2024 (CAD $2.4B AI investment). Provincial laws fill gaps: Ontario Bill 194 requires AI disclosure in hiring.
Policy Lever: Federal voluntary standards through Treasury Board guidelines and AI Impact Assessment tool. Provincial binding laws (Ontario Bill 194, Quebec Law 25). AI Safety Institute evaluates frontier models. No federal binding legislation.
Governance Type: No federal AI regulator. Office of the Privacy Commissioner handles data aspects. Canadian AI Safety Institute provides evaluation. Provincial regulators provide fragmented oversight.
Actor Involvement: Co-chairs Global Partnership on AI (GPAI). Active in OECD, G7, International Network of AI Safety Institutes. Hosts a national AI Safety Institute. Strong international engagement despite domestic legislative gap.
Enforcement Level: Privacy commissioners enforce data protection as applied to AI. Provincial regulators implement provincial AI laws. No federal AI enforcement body.
Specific Laws: Ontario Bill 194 (2025)|Quebec Law 25|Treasury Board AI Guidelines|PIPEDA

Regulation Status: Cape Verde has no AI-specific legislation, regulatory framework, or national AI strategy as of April 2026. The country's approach to AI governance remains limited to participation in continental and regional initiatives through the African Union and Smart Africa Alliance.
Policy Lever: No dedicated AI policy instruments or mechanisms exist. Cape Verde relies on general ICT and digital governance frameworks without AI-specific provisions.
Governance Type: No formal AI governance structures, regulatory bodies, or institutional frameworks have been established. AI oversight is absent at the national level.
Actor Involvement: Cape Verde participates passively in the African Union and Smart Africa Alliance initiatives but maintains limited bilateral AI engagement with other nations. Domestic actors in government, industry, and academia show minimal coordinated involvement in AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, compliance mechanisms, or regulatory oversight bodies exist for AI-related activities.

Regulation Status: The Central African Republic has no AI-specific governance framework or regulatory activity as of April 2026. Ongoing political instability and institutional weakness continue to prevent any meaningful AI policy development or engagement.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments or mechanisms are in place. The country lacks the institutional capacity to develop or implement AI regulations.
Governance Type: No formal AI governance structures exist. Political instability and limited state capacity preclude the establishment of regulatory bodies or oversight mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: The Central African Republic maintains nominal membership in the African Union but does not actively participate in continental AI policy initiatives. No domestic or international AI policy engagement is documented.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms for AI regulation exist given the absence of any regulatory framework.

Regulation Status: Chad has no AI-specific governance framework or regulatory approach as of May 2026. The military government has not initiated AI policy development, national strategy, or engagement with international AI governance initiatives. AI regulation remains entirely absent from the country's policy landscape.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments, guidelines, regulatory mechanisms, or governance tools have been established in Chad. AI is not addressed through existing sectoral regulation, data protection frameworks, or any indirect policy levers.
Governance Type: No dedicated governance structures, institutional bodies, or regulatory authorities for AI oversight exist in Chad. AI regulation is not integrated into any existing regulatory framework or institutional mandate.
Actor Involvement: Chad maintains nominal membership in the African Union but has not engaged in AU AI governance initiatives or multilateral AI policy discussions. No domestic government, industry, academic, or civil-society actors are involved in AI policy development processes.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, compliance frameworks, or sanctioning authorities exist for AI regulation in Chad.

Regulation Status: Chile's comprehensive AI bill passed the Chamber of Deputies (August 2025) and is under Senate review. The new data protection law (Law 21.719) takes full effect December 2026. Chile has positioned itself as Latin America's AI governance leader through hosting the Santiago Declaration.
Policy Lever: Draft AI legislation with risk-based classification. Consumer protection circular on AI (2023) provides existing enforcement basis. Data protection law reform strengthens AI governance foundation.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Science coordinates AI policy. Consumer protection agency (SERNAC) applies AI-relevant oversight. Draft legislation would establish a formal AI governance authority.
Actor Involvement: Hosted the Santiago Declaration (2023) establishing Latin American AI ethics framework. OECD member with active AI policy engagement. Leads the Intergovernmental Council on AI for LAC.
Enforcement Level: SERNAC applies consumer protection to AI contexts. Formal AI enforcement mechanisms await legislation passage.
Specific Laws: Draft AI Bill (Senate review 2025)|Consumer Protection AI Circular (2023)|Law 21.719 (Data Protection, 2026)

Regulation Status: China has the world's most extensive AI regulatory framework: Generative AI Service Regulations (2023), Algorithm Recommendation Regulations (2022), AI Content Labeling Measures (effective Sep 2025 with mandatory standard GB 45438-2025), and amended Cybersecurity Law (effective Jan 2026) with AI provisions. Over 100 generative AI services approved by mid-2025.
Policy Lever: Comprehensive instruments including mandatory AI content labeling (dual explicit/implicit), algorithm filing and security assessments, generative AI licensing, data localization, and cybersecurity review. Strategic chip export controls and AI Plus Action Plan (Aug 2025) for cross-sector AI integration.
Governance Type: Multi-agency governance through CAC (Cyberspace Administration), MIIT, SAMR, and TC260 standards body. Algorithm filing system and pre-launch security assessments create comprehensive oversight infrastructure.
Actor Involvement: Leads AI governance through BRICS, Global AI Governance Initiative (30+ countries joined), bilateral agreements, and active ISO/ITU standard-setting. Co-signatory to Bletchley Declaration. Maintains independent regulatory model.
Enforcement Level: World's most active enforcement: two major 2025 Qinglang campaigns handled 3,500+ problematic AI products, removed 960,000+ pieces of illegal content. CAC conducts audits, fines, platform investigations, and content takedowns. Amended Cybersecurity Law enables immediate severe fines (up to RMB 10M).
Specific Laws: Generative AI Service Regulations (2023)|Algorithm Recommendation Regulations (2022)|AI Content Labeling Measures (Sep 2025)|Cybersecurity Law (amended Jan 2026)|PIPL (2021)|Data Security Law (2021)

Regulation Status: Colombia adopted CONPES 4144 (February 2025), a major AI policy document with 106 actions and ~479 billion pesos investment. Bill 422/2025 for binding AI regulation is under congressional review alongside consolidated data protection reform (Bill 274/2025).
Policy Lever: CONPES policy framework with detailed action plan. Draft legislation under review. Existing data protection law (Law 1581/2012) provides indirect governance.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation coordinates AI policy. The SIC (data protection authority) handles AI-related data complaints. No dedicated AI regulatory body.
Actor Involvement: Active in Santiago Declaration, Cartagena Declaration, and OECD AI policy discussions. Colombia is an OECD accession country with growing international AI engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement. SIC handles data protection complaints related to AI applications.
Specific Laws: CONPES 4144 (Feb 2025)|Draft Bill 422/2025|Law 1581/2012 (Data Protection)

Regulation Status: Comoros has no AI governance framework or regulatory activity as of April 2026. The country lacks any formal AI policy, legislative proposals, or strategic initiatives addressing artificial intelligence regulation.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments or mechanisms exist for AI governance in Comoros. The country has not adopted guidelines, standards, or regulatory frameworks.
Governance Type: No dedicated governance structures or institutional bodies for AI oversight have been established in Comoros. No ministry, agency, or inter-agency task force addresses AI policy.
Actor Involvement: Comoros maintains nominal membership in the African Union, which has adopted continental AI strategies, but the country itself has not engaged in substantive participation or implementation of AU AI initiatives. Domestic actors have not mobilized around AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, oversight bodies, or compliance monitoring systems exist for AI regulation in Comoros.

Regulation Status: The Republic of Congo has no AI-specific legislation, regulatory framework, or formal governance structures for artificial intelligence as of April 2026. AI regulation remains absent from national policy priorities.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments, guidelines, or regulatory mechanisms are in place. Congo lacks dedicated policy levers for AI governance.
Governance Type: No AI governance structures, agencies, or institutional bodies exist. Congo has not established dedicated governance mechanisms for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Congo maintains nominal African Union membership but demonstrates no active engagement in continental or multilateral AI policy initiatives. Domestic actors show minimal involvement in AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as no regulatory framework has been established.

Regulation Status: Costa Rica has a drafted AI regulation bill and is the most advanced Central American country on AI governance. It adheres to OECD AI Principles and participates actively in regional AI cooperation.
Policy Lever: Draft AI legislation and OECD AI Principles adherence. Existing data protection and consumer protection laws provide some indirect governance.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Science coordinates AI policy. Costa Rica's OECD membership drives institutional alignment with international AI governance standards.
Actor Involvement: OECD member with active AI policy participation. Leads Central American engagement in Santiago and Cartagena Declaration frameworks. Hosted regional AI governance workshops.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement pending legislation.
Specific Laws: Draft AI Regulation Bill|OECD AI Principles (adherent)

Regulation Status: Croatia implements the EU AI Act. The Personal Data Protection Agency (AZOP) is expected to lead national implementation, which is progressing.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act risk-based framework. No significant supplementary national AI policy instruments.
Governance Type: AZOP designated as lead authority with sector coordination being established.
Actor Involvement: Participates in EU AI Board and Southeast European digital cooperation frameworks.
Enforcement Level: AZOP has enforcement authority under EU AI Act. AI-specific enforcement experience developing.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)

Regulation Status: Cuba has no AI-specific legislation or regulatory framework as of April 2026. AI governance remains integrated into general state technology administration rather than through dedicated legal instruments. The limited digital infrastructure and state control of technology sectors continue to constrain AI deployment and the perceived need for specialized regulation.
Policy Lever: Cuba relies on state administrative control through general technology oversight mechanisms rather than AI-specific policy instruments. No dedicated regulatory bodies or policy levers for AI governance have been established.
Governance Type: Cuba maintains centralized state control of technology sectors without dedicated AI governance structures. AI-related activities are subject to general government administrative oversight rather than specialized institutional frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Cuba signed the Santiago Declaration on AI in 2023, representing limited international engagement on AI policy. Domestic involvement remains constrained by economic limitations and restricted internet access, with engagement primarily through state-controlled channels.
Enforcement Level: No specific enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, though general state technology oversight may apply to AI-related activities.

Regulation Status: Cyprus implements the EU AI Act. The Office of the Commissioner for Personal Data Protection leads implementation within the island's small regulatory apparatus.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act risk-based framework. Limited additional national instruments.
Governance Type: Commissioner for Personal Data Protection leads. Small regulatory apparatus relies heavily on EU-level guidance.
Actor Involvement: Participates in EU AI Board. Cyprus's services hub status gives AI regulation practical importance.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement authority exists under EU AI Act. Practical capacity is developing.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)|Cyprus Data Protection Law

Regulation Status: Czechia implements the EU AI Act and has been proactive in AI governance, having hosted EU Council Presidency discussions that advanced the AI Act negotiations. National implementation is well-advanced.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act framework supplemented by national digital strategy and innovation sandbox initiatives.
Governance Type: UOOU (data protection office) designated as competent authority. Government Council for Information Society coordinates inter-ministerial AI policy.
Actor Involvement: Active in EU AI Board, OECD, and Central European digital cooperation. Czech leadership shaped key AI Act provisions during Council Presidency.
Enforcement Level: UOOU exercises enforcement powers. Active compliance monitoring through established data protection infrastructure.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)

Regulation Status: Côte d'Ivoire lacks dedicated AI legislation as of April 2026. AI governance remains embedded within the broader digital transformation strategy adopted by the government, with no comprehensive standalone AI policy or regulatory framework.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms rely primarily on the national digital transformation strategy and general data protection provisions rather than AI-specific regulations. Indirect governance occurs through existing digital economy frameworks coordinated by the Ministry of Communication and Digital Economy.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Communication and Digital Economy holds responsibility for digital policy coordination but has no dedicated AI governance body or regulatory agency. Governance remains decentralized across general digital policy structures without AI-specific institutional arrangements.
Actor Involvement: Côte d'Ivoire participates in the African Union's Continental AI Strategy and the Smart Africa Alliance, indicating regional engagement. Bilateral AI governance engagement remains limited, with focus concentrated on continental African initiatives rather than bilateral arrangements.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms exist; oversight relies on general digital governance bodies without dedicated authority to monitor or enforce AI-related compliance.

Regulation Status: The DRC has no AI-specific governance framework or regulatory activity as of April 2026. Persistent institutional weakness, ongoing conflict, and limited digital infrastructure continue to preclude AI policy development.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments or mechanisms exist. General ICT regulations remain underdeveloped.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structures or responsible agencies have been established. Digital policy oversight remains fragmented across weak institutional bodies.
Actor Involvement: The DRC maintains nominal membership in the African Union but demonstrates no active participation in AU AI initiatives or regional AI governance efforts. Domestic engagement with AI policy remains negligible.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms for AI regulation exist due to absence of applicable legislation and limited state capacity.

Regulation Status: Denmark was among the first EU states to adopt national AI Act implementing legislation. Datatilsynet leads enforcement and has published extensive AI compliance guidance since 2024.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act combined with proactive national AI guidance for public sector deployment. Specific guidelines for AI in government services.
Governance Type: Datatilsynet leads with strong institutional capacity. Agency for Digital Government coordinates public sector AI governance through established digital strategy mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Leads Nordic AI cooperation. Active in EU AI Board, OECD, GPAI. Strong multi-stakeholder engagement.
Enforcement Level: Datatilsynet actively enforces data protection as applied to AI with established penalty procedures and audit programs.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)|Danish Data Protection Act

Regulation Status: Djibouti has no dedicated AI governance framework or regulatory activity as of April 2026. The country has not established specific legislation, national AI strategies, or regulatory bodies focused on artificial intelligence oversight.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidelines, or regulatory mechanisms are in place. Djibouti relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks if any exist, but does not employ targeted AI policy levers.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structures or institutional bodies have been established. AI oversight responsibilities, if any, remain uncoordinated across general government agencies.
Actor Involvement: Djibouti shows minimal engagement with international AI governance initiatives, standard-setting bodies, or multilateral digital policy forums. Domestic stakeholder involvement in AI policy development is not documented.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation due to the absence of any regulatory framework.

Regulation Status: Dominica has no dedicated AI legislation, regulatory framework, or national AI strategy as of April 2026. The country's engagement with AI governance remains limited to participation in the Santiago Declaration through CARICOM regional mechanisms.

Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structures, oversight bodies, or institutional frameworks exist within Dominica's government. AI-related matters are not assigned to any specific ministry or agency.
Actor Involvement: Dominica participates in the Santiago Declaration as a CARICOM member state, representing minimal regional coordination on AI policy. No domestic AI policy actors, advisory bodies, or stakeholder engagement mechanisms have been established.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, monitoring bodies, or penalty structures exist for AI-related activities, as the country lacks regulatory frameworks to enforce.

Regulation Status: The Dominican Republic has no binding AI legislation as of April 2026, but demonstrates growing institutional interest through hosting the 3rd Ministerial Summit on AI Ethics and participation in regional declarations. The regulatory approach remains primarily advisory and soft-policy focused.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to soft instruments including regional declarations (Santiago/Cartagena Declarations) and summit-based coordination. The country relies on voluntary ethical frameworks rather than enforceable legal requirements.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance body exists; digital policy coordination falls under the Ministry of Industry. Governance remains decentralized without a specialized agency focused on AI regulation.
Actor Involvement: The Dominican Republic hosts the 3rd Ministerial Summit on AI Ethics and participates in regional Latin American governance frameworks through Santiago and Cartagena Declarations. Involvement is primarily at the ministerial and regional diplomatic level.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist, as the regulatory framework consists solely of voluntary commitments and soft policy declarations without legal backing or monitoring bodies.

Regulation Status: East Timor has no AI governance activity. Basic digital infrastructure constraints make AI governance a distant priority.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments.
Governance Type: No governance structures.
Actor Involvement: Minimal international digital policy engagement.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement.

Regulation Status: Ecuador has not enacted AI-specific legislation as of April 2026, continuing to rely on general data protection frameworks and indirect governance mechanisms. The country participates in regional Latin American AI governance discussions but lacks a comprehensive domestic AI regulatory framework.
Policy Lever: Ecuador applies general data protection law (Organic Law of Personal Data Protection) and telecommunications regulations as indirect tools for AI governance. No dedicated AI-specific policy instruments or sectoral guidelines have been established.
Governance Type: Ecuador has not established a dedicated AI governance body. Digital policy matters remain dispersed across the General Superintendence of Telecommunications (SUPERTEL) and general data protection authorities without centralized AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Ecuador participates in regional Latin American AI governance frameworks and maintains OECD engagement on digital policy but has minimal domestic multi-stakeholder AI governance structures. International participation occurs without corresponding domestic institutional development.
Enforcement Level: No specific enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, with only indirect enforcement through general data protection and telecommunications authorities lacking dedicated AI compliance oversight.

Regulation Status: Egypt launched its second National AI Strategy (2025-2030) in January 2025, targeting $42.7 billion in annual AI value and 30,000 specialists by 2030. An AI law is under parliamentary development. The National Council for AI coordinates strategy execution.
Policy Lever: National AI strategy with investment targets and sector-specific plans. Draft AI legislation under development. NTRA (telecom authority) provides sectoral oversight.
Governance Type: National Council for AI coordinates strategy. NTRA handles telecom-adjacent AI regulation. Egyptian AI Institute provides research support.
Actor Involvement: Active in AU AI governance. Hosted Africa AI events. Engages with OECD, UNESCO AI readiness assessments. Strong Middle East/Africa regional presence.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement yet. Existing telecom and consumer protection authorities handle complaints tangentially.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy 2025-2030|Draft AI Law (under parliamentary development)

Regulation Status: El Salvador has no AI-specific legislation or regulatory framework as of April 2026. The government continues to prioritize Bitcoin and cryptocurrency adoption over AI governance, with minimal domestic policy activity in the AI domain.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments or mechanisms are currently in place. El Salvador lacks dedicated AI policy levers, guidelines, or strategic frameworks.
Governance Type: El Salvador has no established AI governance structures or institutional bodies responsible for AI oversight. No dedicated agencies or committees address AI regulation.
Actor Involvement: El Salvador is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration on AI in the Americas but shows minimal domestic policy engagement. Limited involvement from government, academia, or private sector in coordinated AI governance initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms or oversight bodies exist for AI-related matters.

Regulation Status: Equatorial Guinea has no documented AI governance framework or regulatory activity as of April 2026. The country lacks any AI-specific legislation, national strategy, or regulatory agency dedicated to AI oversight.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments, guidelines, or legislative mechanisms are in place. The country has not adopted voluntary standards or regulatory frameworks for AI governance.
Governance Type: No formal governance structures exist for AI regulation. There are no designated agencies, regulatory bodies, or institutional frameworks responsible for AI oversight in Equatorial Guinea.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is limited to nominal African Union membership with no active participation in AU AI initiatives. No domestic stakeholders, international partnerships, or coordinated governance efforts on AI have been documented.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, compliance monitoring, or regulatory oversight capacity exists for AI-related activities.

Regulation Status: Eritrea has no AI governance framework or regulatory activity as of 2026. The country's severe restrictions on internet access, limited technological infrastructure, and international isolation continue to preclude any AI policy development or regulatory engagement.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments, guidelines, or legislative mechanisms exist in Eritrea. The country has not adopted any formal policy levers related to AI governance.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structures or institutional bodies have been established. Eritrea lacks the administrative and technical capacity for AI-specific oversight.
Actor Involvement: Minimal domestic and international engagement on AI policy. Eritrea does not participate in multilateral AI governance forums or international AI regulation initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation given the absence of any regulatory framework.

Regulation Status: Estonia implements the EU AI Act and leverages its world-leading digital governance infrastructure (e-Residency, X-Road) for AI oversight. The Kratt strategy guides public sector AI adoption.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act integrated with Estonia's e-governance ecosystem. AI Impact Assessments required for public sector AI. Innovation-friendly regulatory sandbox approach.
Governance Type: RIA (Information System Authority) and Data Protection Inspectorate share AI oversight. Estonia's advanced digital governance infrastructure supports sophisticated AI regulation.
Actor Involvement: Internationally recognized digital governance leader. Active in EU AI Board, OECD, NATO AI governance, and International Network of AI Safety Institutes discussions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement leverages Estonia's established digital oversight infrastructure with monitoring and compliance mechanisms.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)|Kratt AI Strategy

Regulation Status: Ethiopia established the autonomous Ethiopian AI Institute (EAII) in 2022 with authority to certify AI products and adopted a National AI Policy in June 2024.
Policy Lever: National AI policy and institutional framework. EAII has product certification authority but binding regulatory implementation is limited.
Governance Type: Ethiopian AI Institute (EAII) is one of Africa's most established dedicated AI bodies, with certification authority and research mandate.
Actor Involvement: Engages through AU Continental AI Strategy. Part of Smart Africa Alliance. Growing bilateral AI cooperation.
Enforcement Level: EAII has certification authority but enforcement mechanisms are nascent.
Specific Laws: National AI Policy (June 2024)|Ethiopian AI Institute (est. 2022)

Regulation Status: Fiji has embedded AI considerations into its National Development Plan 2024-2029 and is actively developing a dedicated AI governance framework. However, no binding AI-specific legislation has been enacted as of April 2026, with oversight currently indirect through cybersecurity frameworks.
Policy Lever: AI policy is integrated into national development planning and coordinated through digital policy initiatives. Indirect governance occurs via existing cybersecurity frameworks, with the AI governance framework still under development.
Governance Type: Fiji lacks a dedicated AI regulatory body; the Department of Communications coordinates digital and emerging AI policy. Governance remains distributed across existing departments without a centralized AI authority.
Actor Involvement: Fiji is the most active Pacific Island nation on AI governance, participating in Pacific Islands Forum digital discussions and regional AI initiatives. National-level coordination involves government departments and international engagement on AI standards.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanism for AI governance exists; compliance remains voluntary pending finalization of the governance framework.

Regulation Status: Finland was the first EU member state to achieve full AI Act enforcement powers (laws approved December 22, 2025, effective January 1, 2026), appointing 10 existing market surveillance authorities. National AI programme updated since 2017.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act with full national implementing legislation. Innovation sandboxes, public procurement AI standards, and the Elements of AI citizen education programme (1% of population trained).
Governance Type: Ministry of Transport and Communications coordinates. Data Protection Ombudsman leads enforcement. Ten market surveillance authorities designated for sector-specific AI oversight—most comprehensive EU designation.
Actor Involvement: Frontrunner in EU AI governance. AuroraAI human-centric AI initiative. Active in OECD, GPAI, Nordic AI cooperation. Elements of AI is a global model for AI literacy.
Enforcement Level: Full enforcement powers operational since January 2026. Data Protection Ombudsman and 10 designated authorities conduct compliance monitoring.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|Finnish AI Act Implementation (Dec 2025)|GDPR (2018)

Regulation Status: France implements the EU AI Act with CNIL as lead competent authority. Hosted the AI Action Summit (Feb 2025, successor to Bletchley Park). CNIL has the most AI audit experience of any European DPA.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act combined with CNIL's active algorithmic accountability enforcement and national AI strategy (€1.5B public investment). Sector-specific guidance for healthcare, finance, and public administration.
Governance Type: CNIL leads with established AI audit expertise (algorithm audit methodology since 2017). National AI coordinator and inter-ministerial coordination. CNIL published binding algorithmic transparency recommendations.
Actor Involvement: Global AI governance leader: hosted 2025 AI Action Summit (61 signatories), co-chairs GPAI, active in OECD. CNIL is one of the most influential AI regulators worldwide.
Enforcement Level: CNIL has issued enforcement decisions on algorithmic processing. Active audit programme with AI-specific methodology. No AI Act-specific fines yet (none issued EU-wide).
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)|Loi Informatique et Libertés

Regulation Status: Gabon has no dedicated AI governance framework or regulatory instruments as of April 2026. The country has not initiated formal AI-specific legislation or national AI strategy.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy mechanisms or instruments have been implemented. General ICT and data protection frameworks do not specifically address AI systems.
Governance Type: No formal AI governance structure exists. Gabon lacks dedicated institutional bodies or regulatory agencies responsible for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Gabon maintains nominal membership in the African Union but has not actively participated in AU AI governance initiatives or regional AI policy development. Limited domestic stakeholder engagement in AI policy discussions.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as no AI-specific laws or policies have been established.

Regulation Status: Gambia has no dedicated AI governance framework or regulatory activity as of April 2026. The country lacks national AI strategy, legislation, or policy instruments addressing artificial intelligence.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy mechanisms or instruments are in place. Gambia relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks without AI-targeted provisions.
Governance Type: No formal AI governance structure exists. Regulatory authority for AI-related matters is not designated to any specific institution or agency.
Actor Involvement: Gambia maintains nominal membership in the African Union, which has issued AI policy frameworks, but local implementation and adoption remain absent. Engagement with international AI governance initiatives is minimal.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms for AI governance exist due to the absence of regulatory framework and designated oversight bodies.

Regulation Status: Georgia adopted a national AI strategy and is developing regulatory frameworks through EU association alignment. No comprehensive binding AI legislation enacted.
Policy Lever: National AI strategy and EU alignment through the Association Agreement. General data protection framework provides indirect AI governance.
Governance Type: Innovation and Technology Agency (GITA) coordinates AI policy with the Ministry of Economy. No dedicated AI regulatory body.
Actor Involvement: Engages through EU association, Council of Europe AI Convention (signed), and Eastern Partnership digital cooperation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy|Council of Europe AI Convention (signed)

Regulation Status: Germany's draft AI Act implementing law (KI-MIG) was adopted by the federal cabinet on February 10, 2026, designating the Bundesnetzagentur as central authority. An AI Service Desk for SMEs is operational. Delayed by snap elections but advancing.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act with detailed national implementation (KI-MIG). Bundesnetzagentur AI Service Desk provides practical compliance guidance. Sector-specific AI regulation in finance (BaFin) and healthcare.
Governance Type: Bundesnetzagentur designated as central authority under KI-MIG. BaFin (finance), BMG (health), and state data protection authorities share sectoral oversight. Multi-level federal-state governance.
Actor Involvement: Leading voice in EU AI policy. Active in G7 Hiroshima Process, OECD, GPAI. German AI Association and research institutes (DFKI, Fraunhofer) participate extensively in governance.
Enforcement Level: Bundesnetzagentur has enforcement powers under KI-MIG. BaFin enforces financial sector AI rules. Germany's established regulatory culture supports compliance monitoring.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|KI-MIG (draft adopted Feb 2026)|GDPR (2018)|BDSG

Regulation Status: Ghana lacks dedicated AI-specific legislation as of April 2026, relying instead on indirect governance through the Data Protection Act (2012) enforced by the Data Protection Commission. The country is pursuing major investment initiatives, particularly the $1 billion UAE-backed AI hub, which suggests future regulatory development may follow infrastructure deployment.
Policy Lever: Ghana's AI governance relies primarily on the Data Protection Act (2012) and references within the National ICT Strategy, with no dedicated AI legislation or binding regulatory framework. Policy development is largely driven by international engagement and investment initiatives rather than formal legislative measures.
Governance Type: Ghana's AI governance is fragmented across the Data Protection Commission (data aspects), Ministry of Communications (digital policy coordination), and various investment bodies, with no dedicated national AI regulatory authority. The absence of a centralized AI governance structure limits coordinated policy implementation.
Actor Involvement: Ghana is actively engaged in African Union AI governance frameworks and the Smart Africa Alliance, demonstrating regional integration in AI policy discussions. The $1 billion UAE-backed AI hub initiative signals significant international partnerships, though domestic stakeholder coordination structures remain underdeveloped.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is limited to indirect mechanisms through the Data Protection Commission's oversight of data protection aspects; no dedicated AI enforcement body or mechanism exists, resulting in minimal active enforcement of AI-specific requirements.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act, 2012

Regulation Status: Greece implements the EU AI Act with the Hellenic Data Protection Authority (HDPA) leading. National AI strategy adopted in 2024 complements EU obligations.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act framework combined with national AI strategy. Limited additional instruments beyond EU requirements.
Governance Type: HDPA leads with Ministry of Digital Governance coordination. National AI strategy includes public sector deployment provisions.
Actor Involvement: Participates in EU AI Board and Mediterranean digital cooperation. Growing AI research community engagement.
Enforcement Level: HDPA has enforcement authority. AI-specific enforcement experience developing.

Regulation Status: Grenada has no AI-specific legislation, policy framework, or regulatory approach as of April 2026. The country has not enacted any AI laws or binding regulations.

Regulation Status: Honduras has not enacted AI-specific legislation as of April 2026. The country's regulatory approach remains minimal, with engagement limited to international commitments such as the Santiago Declaration on AI.
Policy Lever: No formal policy mechanisms or instruments for AI regulation have been implemented. Honduras relies on existing general technology and consumer protection frameworks without AI-specific provisions.

Policy Lever: EU AI Act framework. No significant supplementary national AI policy instruments.
Governance Type: NAIH (data protection authority) expected to lead. Formal designation delayed.

Regulation Status: Iceland will apply the EU AI Act through the EEA Agreement once formally incorporated (pending). Persónuvernd leads preparation.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act framework (pending EEA incorporation) combined with national data protection enforcement.
Governance Type: Persónuvernd leads with Nordic cooperation support. Compact institutional structure enables efficient coordination.
Actor Involvement: Active in EEA/EFTA governance, Nordic DPA cooperation, OECD AI discussions.
