Regulation Status: India has deliberately chosen not to enact comprehensive AI legislation, instead releasing non-binding AI Governance Guidelines (November 2025) built around seven 'Sutras.' The IndiaAI Mission provides 10,000+ GPUs. An IndiaAI Safety Institute follows a hub-and-spoke model with sectoral regulators.
Policy Lever: Non-binding seven Sutras guidelines, DPDP Act 2023 (and 2025 Rules) as primary legal framework, sector-specific regulation through RBI, SEBI, and healthcare regulators. IndiaAI Mission provides compute infrastructure.
Governance Type: IndiaAI Safety Institute coordinates with sectoral regulators (RBI, SEBI, IRDAI) through hub-and-spoke model. MeitY coordinates national AI policy. No standalone AI regulator.
Actor Involvement: Active in G20 (hosted 2023 presidency), BRICS, GPAI. Part of International Network of AI Safety Institutes discussions. IndiaAI Safety Institute established.
Enforcement Level: Sector regulators (RBI, SEBI) enforce AI-relevant rules within their domains. DPDP Act enforcement mechanisms applying to AI data practices. No standalone AI enforcement.
Specific Laws: Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023|IndiaAI Mission|AI Governance Guidelines (Nov 2025)

Regulation Status: Indonesia is finalizing a presidential regulation (Perpres) on AI ethics and safety for early-mid 2026. The Philippines' 2026 ASEAN chairmanship may accelerate regional binding governance that affects Indonesia.
Policy Lever: Draft presidential regulation on AI ethics/safety. GR 71/2019 on electronic systems provides indirect governance. ASEAN AI governance guide alignment.
Governance Type: Ministry of Communication and Digital coordinates AI policy. BRIN (research agency) provides technical input. No dedicated AI regulatory body.
Actor Involvement: Active in ASEAN AI governance framework. Growing bilateral AI partnerships with Japan, South Korea, and UAE.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement. KOMINFO handles general digital compliance.
Specific Laws: Draft Presidential Regulation on AI Ethics|GR 71/2019|ASEAN AI Governance Guide (2024)

Regulation Status: Iran has established institutional frameworks for AI governance through the National AI Organization (NAIO) created by parliamentary vote in October 2025 and published a National AI Roadmap in May 2025. However, comprehensive binding AI legislation remains in draft or development phase, with regulatory approach focused on strategic advancement rather than restrictive governance.
Policy Lever: Primary policy mechanisms include the National AI Roadmap with $8 billion investment targets and NAIO coordination across government agencies. Practical deployment of facial recognition and surveillance systems demonstrates active policy implementation in security sectors.
Governance Type: NAIO functions as a dedicated national AI governance body established by legislation, coordinating with the presidency and security apparatus. This represents one of the more formal institutional AI governance structures globally, though integrated primarily with domestic security and strategic technology objectives.
Actor Involvement: Iran operates largely outside international AI governance frameworks due to sanctions, instead pursuing bilateral cooperation with China and Russia including a Global AI Governance Initiative launched with China in March 2024. Domestic actors include government ministries, security services, and research institutions aligned with state strategic objectives.
Enforcement Level: Limited public transparency on enforcement mechanisms; oversight appears concentrated within state security and presidency structures without evidence of independent regulatory bodies or formal penalty frameworks for AI misuse.
Specific Laws: National AI Organization (established October 2025)

Regulation Status: Iraq continues to operate without enacted AI legislation as of May 2026, relying instead on the draft National AI Strategy Initiative (INSAI) which remains non-binding. Political instability and institutional fragmentation have prevented progression from early-stage strategic planning to formal regulatory frameworks.
Policy Lever: Iraq's primary policy mechanism is the unenacted INSAI draft strategy, which lacks binding enforcement mechanisms or operational implementation structures. No dedicated legislation, executive orders, sectoral guidance, or regulatory instruments governing AI deployment have been formally adopted.
Governance Type: Iraq lacks a centralized AI governance body or dedicated regulatory authority for artificial intelligence. Oversight responsibility remains fragmented across multiple ministries without formal coordination mechanisms or clear institutional accountability for AI policy.
Actor Involvement: Iraq has limited engagement with international AI governance initiatives and minimal participation in regional AI policy coordination. Governance development has remained largely within government circles with minimal structured consultation with industry, academia, or civil society.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist; the draft strategy contains no sanctioning framework, and no authority has been tasked with compliance oversight or penalty administration.

Regulation Status: Ireland published the General Scheme of its AI Bill on February 4, 2026, establishing 15 competent authorities and a National AI Office by August 2026. S.I. No. 366/2025 designated initial authorities. As EU headquarters for Meta, Google, TikTok, Ireland's enforcement has global significance.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act plus national AI Bill (General Scheme Feb 2026) establishing 15 competent authorities. DPC's tech company oversight experience is unmatched.
Governance Type: DPC leads enforcement with established experience regulating major tech companies. National AI Office being established. S.I. 366/2025 designated initial coordination mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Ireland's role as European tech hub gives it leading influence globally. Active in EU AI Board, OECD, international enforcement cooperation.
Enforcement Level: DPC is one of the most active DPAs globally and extends this to AI. Its decisions on Meta, Google, and TikTok AI practices have cross-border impact.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024)|AI Bill General Scheme (Feb 2026)|S.I. 366/2025|GDPR (2018)

Regulation Status: Israel follows a sector-based policy approach. The December 2023 AI Policy sets 'Responsible Innovation' as the guiding principle. The Privacy Protection Authority gained expanded powers under Amendment 13 (effective August 2025) and conducts proactive AI system audits.
Policy Lever: Sector-based regulation through PPA, financial regulators, and competition authority. Amendment 13 strengthens automated decision-making oversight. Soft regulation through policy documents and guidance.
Governance Type: Privacy Protection Authority leads with expanded powers. AI Policy Coordination Center being established by early 2026. Sector regulators maintain domain-specific AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Active in OECD, bilateral AI partnerships. Israel's technology sector gives practical importance to AI governance. PPA conducts proactive compliance audits of high-risk AI systems.
Enforcement Level: PPA conducts proactive AI compliance audits under expanded Amendment 13 powers. Sector regulators enforce within their domains. No standalone AI enforcement body.
Specific Laws: AI Policy: Responsible Innovation (Dec 2023)|Privacy Protection Amendment 13 (Aug 2025)

Regulation Status: Italy enacted the first national AI law in the EU—Law 132/2025 (effective October 10, 2025)—covering healthcare, employment, justice, and IP. New criminal offenses for deepfakes carry 1-5 years imprisonment. A €1 billion AI venture fund was established.
Policy Lever: EU AI Act plus Law 132/2025 with criminal penalties, enhanced market manipulation fines, and sector-specific provisions. Italy's approach is notably stricter than EU minimum requirements.
Governance Type: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali as primary authority. AgID oversees public sector AI. ACN handles cybersecurity AI. Italy has one of the most institutional AI governance architectures in Europe.
Actor Involvement: Italy chairs G7 AI governance, played leading role in EU AI Act negotiations. Garante was the first authority to act against ChatGPT (March 2023). €1B venture fund signals serious commitment.
Enforcement Level: Garante enforces with administrative fines plus criminal penalties under Law 132/2025 (1-5 years for deepfakes). Among the strictest enforcement regimes globally.
Specific Laws: Law 132/2025 (Italian AI Law)|EU AI Act (2024)|GDPR (2018)

Regulation Status: Jamaica has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of April 2026. The country continues to rely primarily on the Data Protection Act (2020) for indirect governance of AI-related activities. No comprehensive national AI policy or regulatory framework has been formally established.
Policy Lever: Jamaica employs no dedicated AI policy instruments. The Data Protection Act (2020) provides limited indirect governance through data protection mechanisms rather than AI-specific regulation.
Governance Type: Jamaica has not established a dedicated AI governance body or regulatory authority. Oversight responsibility remains dispersed across existing institutions with data protection responsibilities.
Actor Involvement: Jamaica participates in regional AI governance discussions through CARICOM and is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration on AI ethics. Limited domestic stakeholder coordination structures exist for AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms exist for AI-specific regulation; enforcement is limited to data protection compliance under the 2020 Act.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act, 2020

Regulation Status: Japan enacted the AI Promotion Act (May 28, 2025)—a principles-based law establishing an AI Strategy Headquarters under the PM. Companies can be publicly named for AI-related human rights violations. No direct penalties but existing sector laws apply.
Policy Lever: AI Promotion Act enables name-and-shame enforcement. Strategic semiconductor export controls are a major policy lever. R&D investment and NIST-equivalent standards provide additional governance.
Governance Type: AI Strategy Headquarters under the PM coordinates policy. Japan AI Safety Institute (AISI) operational since February 2024. Multi-ministry coordination through MIC, METI, and Cabinet Office.
Actor Involvement: Leads international AI governance through G7 Hiroshima AI Process, co-hosts AI Safety Summits. Part of International Network of AI Safety Institutes. GPAI participant. Global semiconductor governance leader.
Enforcement Level: Name-and-shame mechanism under AI Promotion Act. Sector regulators (FSA, MIC) enforce within domains. AISI conducts model evaluations. No direct fines under AI Promotion Act.
Specific Laws: AI Promotion Act (May 2025)|APPI|Social Principles for Human-Centric AI (2019)

Regulation Status: Jordan has a National AI Strategy 2023-2027 with 68 projects across 12 sectors and a National AI Code of Ethics under development. No binding legislation enacted.
Policy Lever: Strategy-based approach with 68 planned projects. Draft AI Code of Ethics. No binding regulatory instruments.
Governance Type: National AI strategy coordination through government agencies. No dedicated regulatory body.
Actor Involvement: Active in Middle East AI discussions. Bilateral cooperation with UAE and European partners on AI capacity building.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms. Strategy in implementation phase.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy 2023-2027|Draft AI Code of Ethics

Regulation Status: Kazakhstan enacted the first comprehensive AI law in Central Asia—Law No. 230-VIII (signed November 17, 2025, effective January 18, 2026)—covering developer, owner, and user obligations with seven guiding principles. A Ministry of AI and Digital Development was established September 2025.
Policy Lever: Comprehensive AI law with developer/owner/user obligations. Seven guiding principles framework. Alem.ai international AI center targets $5B in AI solution exports by 2029.
Governance Type: Ministry of AI and Digital Development (established Sep 2025)—one of the world's first dedicated AI ministries. Alem.ai center provides research and development coordination.
Actor Involvement: Leads Central Asian AI governance. CIS regional cooperation on AI standards. Growing international partnerships including with Gulf states.
Enforcement Level: Law 230-VIII establishes obligations. Ministry has oversight powers. Enforcement mechanisms being operationalized following January 2026 entry into force.
Specific Laws: Law No. 230-VIII (Nov 2025, effective Jan 2026)

Regulation Status: Kenya's AI Bill 2026 was introduced to the Senate on February 19, 2026—the closest to becoming law on the African continent. It proposes a three-tier governance structure (AI Commissioner, AI Authority, Advisory Council), mandatory registration for high-risk systems, and criminal penalties.
Policy Lever: Draft AI Bill with risk-based classification and mandatory registration. National AI Strategy promotes innovation alongside governance. Existing data protection law (2019) provides foundation.
Governance Type: Proposed AI Commissioner and AI Authority would create dedicated governance. Currently, ICT Ministry and Office of Data Protection Commissioner coordinate.
Actor Involvement: Leads African AI governance engagement. Hosted inaugural Global AI Summit on Africa (Kigali reference partner). Active in AU, Smart Africa Alliance, and bilateral partnerships.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement yet. Data Protection Commissioner handles AI-related data complaints under the Data Protection Act 2019.
Specific Laws: AI Bill 2026 (Senate introduced Feb 2026)|Data Protection Act 2019

Regulation Status: Kiribati has no AI governance. Focus is on basic connectivity and climate adaptation rather than AI policy.

Regulation Status: Kosovo has no dedicated AI legislation as of May 2026 and remains in the earliest stages of AI policy development. Policy Lever: AI Basic Act establishing principles framework. Risk classification under development. Substantial public investment. Semiconductor governance as strategic policy lever.
Governance Type: NSTC coordinates inter-ministerial AI policy. Ministry of Digital Affairs handles implementation. AI evaluation council to be established under the Act.
Actor Involvement: Active in international AI governance despite diplomatic constraints. Semiconductor supply chain gives unique strategic leverage. Part of like-minded coalition on AI safety.
Enforcement Level: AI Basic Act establishes oversight framework. Enforcement mechanisms being developed alongside risk classification.
Specific Laws: AI Basic Act (Dec 2025, promulgated Jan 2026)

Regulation Status: Tajikistan has adopted a Strategy for AI Development until 2040 and maintains a strategy-based approach without binding legislative instruments. The country has positioned itself as a vocal advocate for equitable AI access through international forums, particularly via its 2025 UN General Assembly resolution, but lacks comprehensive domestic AI legislation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are primarily strategy-based and advocacy-focused, lacking binding regulatory instruments. The approach emphasizes international cooperation and equitable access principles rather than enforceable domestic rules.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI regulatory body exists; AI governance coordination falls under the general ICT ministry without specialized institutional infrastructure. Governance structure remains diffuse across existing government agencies without centralized AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Tajikistan has demonstrated notable international leadership through initiating the July 2025 UN General Assembly resolution on equitable AI access, positioning itself as an advocate for global AI equity. Involvement extends beyond domestic actors to significant international engagement on AI governance principles.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation given the absence of binding legislation; compliance is voluntary and unenforced.

Regulation Status: Tanzania has no national AI strategy or dedicated AI legislation as of April 2026. The country participates in AU-level AI governance discussions and has hosted workshops on AI policy, but regulatory efforts remain largely informal and non-binding.
Policy Lever: Tanzania relies primarily on workshop-based policy development and voluntary participation in regional initiatives rather than binding legal instruments. No formal legislative framework for AI has been enacted.
Governance Type: Tanzania has no dedicated AI governance body or regulatory authority. AI-related matters are loosely coordinated through the ICT ministry within its broader digital policy mandate.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the African Union, Smart Africa Alliance, and Tanzania's ICT ministry. International engagement is primarily through regional African frameworks rather than bilateral agreements.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms or dedicated oversight structures exist for AI governance in Tanzania.

Regulation Status: Thailand has a draft AI law (consolidated from two earlier drafts, public hearing June 2025) adopting a risk-based approach. UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment completed. Active in ASEAN governance.
Policy Lever: Draft AI legislation with risk-based classification. National AI Strategy. UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment completed.
Governance Type: MDES (Ministry of Digital Economy and Society) coordinates. Draft law would establish formal oversight mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Active in ASEAN AI governance. Hosted UNESCO AI Forum discussions. Growing bilateral AI partnerships.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement pending legislation.
Specific Laws: Draft AI Law (public hearing June 2025)|National AI Strategy

Regulation Status: Togo has no dedicated AI regulation framework as of May 2026 and continues to rely on general ICT laws and data protection provisions that do not explicitly address artificial intelligence. AI governance remains absent from national policy documents and legislative priorities.
Policy Lever: Togo employs no AI-specific policy instruments; governance is limited to indirect application of general ICT and data protection frameworks without dedicated AI strategies, guidelines, or sectoral approaches.
Governance Type: Togo lacks formal AI governance structures or designated oversight bodies specifically responsible for artificial intelligence. AI governance remains ad-hoc and embedded within general digital economy initiatives if addressed at all.
Actor Involvement: Togo participates in African Union and ECOWAS regional digital governance initiatives but maintains minimal active coordination on AI policy. Engagement with industry, academia, and civil society on AI matters remains informal and unstructured.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI-specific obligations, as there are no binding AI regulations or designated enforcement authorities.

Regulation Status: Tonga has no AI governance. Focus on basic connectivity and climate resilience.
Policy Lever: No instruments.
Governance Type: No governance.
Actor Involvement: Pacific Islands Forum.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement.

Regulation Status: Trinidad and Tobago has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of April 2026. The country relies on its Data Protection Act for limited indirect governance of AI systems, with no comprehensive AI regulatory framework in place.
Policy Lever: No dedicated AI policy instruments exist. The Data Protection Act (2011) provides minimal indirect governance mechanisms applicable to data processing in AI systems.
Governance Type: Trinidad and Tobago has no dedicated AI governance body or regulatory authority. Data protection oversight falls under the Data Protection Commissioner, but AI-specific governance structures are absent.
Actor Involvement: Trinidad and Tobago participates in CARICOM initiatives and has endorsed the Santiago Declaration on AI ethics. Regional cooperation through CARICOM provides limited framework influence, but no bilateral AI-focused agreements are documented.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms exist; enforcement is limited to general data protection compliance through the Data Protection Commissioner.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act 2011

Regulation Status: Tunisia leads Africa in data and infrastructure readiness and has published AI-relevant sectoral guidelines. No comprehensive AI legislation. The data protection authority (INPDP) provides indirect AI governance.
Policy Lever: Sectoral guidelines and data protection enforcement. Digital strategy references AI.
Governance Type: INPDP handles data aspects. ANSI (digital security) provides cybersecurity oversight. No dedicated AI body.
Actor Involvement: AU engagement. African AI Readiness leader for data/infrastructure. Bilateral partnerships with EU and Gulf states.
Enforcement Level: INPDP enforces data protection. No AI-specific enforcement.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Law|Digital Strategy

Regulation Status: Turkey has a draft AI Bill (submitted June 2024, 8 articles) under parliamentary review, a National AI Strategy 2021-2025, and established a renamed National Technology and AI General Directorate (December 2025) by presidential decree.
Policy Lever: Draft AI Bill with risk-based framework. KVKK data protection enforcement. National AI Strategy provides policy direction.
Governance Type: National Technology and AI General Directorate (est. Dec 2025) by presidential decree—one of few dedicated AI governance bodies globally. BTK and KVKK share oversight.
Actor Involvement: OECD member with active AI governance participation. Council of Europe AI Convention discussions. Bilateral technology cooperation.
Enforcement Level: KVKK enforces data protection with AI relevance. BTK handles telecom AI. Draft law would establish formal AI enforcement.
Specific Laws: Draft AI Bill (June 2024)|National AI Strategy 2021-2025|National AI Directorate (Dec 2025)

Regulation Status: Turkmenistan has no identified AI-specific regulatory frameworks or national AI strategy as of April 2026. The country maintains minimal formal governance structures for emerging technologies, continuing its historical pattern of limited institutional engagement with digital policy development.
Policy Lever: No dedicated policy instruments, legislative acts, or regulatory mechanisms targeting AI have been implemented. Governance of AI remains absent from the policy agenda.
Governance Type: Turkmenistan lacks formal AI governance structures, designated regulatory bodies, or institutional frameworks for technology oversight. No inter-ministerial coordination mechanisms for AI policy have been established.
Actor Involvement: Minimal international engagement in AI policy development or multilateral governance initiatives. Domestic stakeholders show limited participation in global AI governance discussions or regional Central Asian coordination.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation due to the absence of regulatory frameworks.

Regulation Status: Tuvalu has no AI governance. Focus entirely on climate adaptation and basic infrastructure.
Policy Lever: No instruments.
Governance Type: No governance.
Actor Involvement: Minimal engagement.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement.

Regulation Status: Uganda remains in the early stage of AI regulation with only non-binding best practice guidelines released through the ITU summit in 2024. Parliamentary members continue to advocate for dedicated AI legislation, but no formal AI law has been enacted as of April 2026.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to voluntary guidelines and parliamentary advocacy without binding legal instruments. No mandatory compliance frameworks or regulatory requirements are currently in place.
Governance Type: Uganda lacks a dedicated AI regulatory body or institutional structure for AI governance. Parliamentary interest exists but has not translated into formal governance mechanisms or dedicated agencies.
Actor Involvement: International organizations including the African Union, UNDP, and ITU remain engaged in supporting Uganda's AI policy development. Engagement is primarily capacity-building and advisory rather than direct regulatory implementation.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist as Uganda lacks binding AI regulation, relying entirely on voluntary compliance with non-enforceable guidelines.

Regulation Status: Ukraine maintains active AI governance despite conflict: White Paper on AI Regulation (June 2024), two-stage EU alignment roadmap targeting 20% AI Act harmonization by 2026, and voluntary GPAI Code of Conduct adopted by 14 Ukrainian companies.
Policy Lever: White Paper with EU alignment roadmap. Ministry of Digital Transformation guidelines for media AI. Voluntary GPAI Code of Conduct.
Governance Type: Ministry of Digital Transformation leads. Remarkably active institutional development despite conflict.
Actor Involvement: EU alignment discussions, OECD participation, GPAI voluntary code adoption. Active despite conflict.
Enforcement Level: Limited enforcement through ministry oversight. Voluntary compliance mechanisms.
Specific Laws: White Paper on AI Regulation (June 2024)|GPAI Voluntary Code

Regulation Status: The UAE has no standalone federal AI law but adopted a National AI System as an advisory member of all federal entities and Cabinet in January 2026. Minister of State for AI (appointed 2017). PDPL takes full effect Jan 2026 (compliance by Jan 2027). DIFC and ADGM free zones have independent frameworks.
Policy Lever: National AI Strategy 2031. Guidelines and sector-specific frameworks. DIFC AI Licence and ADGM data governance. PDPL provides data foundation.
Governance Type: Minister of State for AI—world's first. National AI System embedded in Cabinet. DIFC and ADGM provide free zone regulation. Most institutionally innovative governance model globally.
Actor Involvement: Global AI governance leader through institutional innovation. Hosts major international AI events. Massive infrastructure investment (Stargate UAE datacenter project).
Enforcement Level: PDPL enforcement beginning. DIFC and ADGM enforce within free zones. No federal AI-specific enforcement mechanism.
Specific Laws: PDPL (Federal Decree-Law 45/2021)|National AI Strategy 2031|National AI System (Jan 2026)|DIFC AI Licence

Regulation Status: The UK has not enacted a single AI law as of March 2026. The AI Bill is not expected before H2 2026. Relies on sector-specific regulation through seven existing regulators. AI Safety Institute rebranded as AI Security Institute (Feb 2025). Declined to sign Paris AI Action Summit declaration.
Policy Lever: Decentralized regulation through ICO, FCA, CMA, Ofcom, MHRA. Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 updates data governance. Five voluntary cross-sector AI principles. No binding AI-specific legislation.
Governance Type: Seven sector regulators apply five principles. DRCF coordination forum. AI Security Institute provides research/evaluation (not enforcement). No single AI regulator.
Actor Involvement: Led through Bletchley Declaration (2023) and AISI, but declined Paris AI Action Summit declaration (Feb 2025) and aligns increasingly with US deregulatory approach.
Enforcement Level: Ofcom most active—fined AI nudification operator, investigated AI character companies. ICO enforces data protection. FCA for financial AI. No dedicated AI enforcement.
Specific Laws: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025|Online Safety Act 2023|Data Protection Act 2018|UK GDPR

Regulation Status: No comprehensive federal AI law. Biden's EO 14110 partially revoked. Trump EO 14365 (Dec 2025) seeks to preempt state AI laws. TAKE IT DOWN Act (May 2025) is the only federal AI statute. States lead: 1,561 AI bills introduced in 2026 across 45 states. California AI Transparency Act, Colorado AI Act, and Texas TRAIGA all effective in 2026.
Policy Lever: Federal: FTC enforcement (Operation AI Comply), SEC AI guidance, NIST RMF. State: California SB 53 Transparency Act (Jan 2026), Colorado AI Act (June 2026), Texas TRAIGA (Jan 2026), Utah AI Policy Act. Export controls on AI chips.
Governance Type: No single federal regulator. FTC, SEC, NIST, state AGs share fragmented oversight. 1,561 state bills create patchwork. Federal-state tension over preemption.
Actor Involvement: Leading global AI actor through bilateral partnerships, G7, OECD. Declined Paris AI Action Summit declaration. Part of International Network of AI Safety Institutes. Semiconductor export controls have global impact.
Enforcement Level: FTC Operation AI Comply continues enforcement against deceptive AI under new administration. State AGs enforce state laws. California AG enforces AI Transparency Act. Federal enforcement limited by deregulatory direction.
Specific Laws: TAKE IT DOWN Act (May 2025)|EO 14365 (Dec 2025)|California AI Transparency Act (Jan 2026)|Colorado AI Act (June 2026)|Texas TRAIGA (Jan 2026)|Utah AI Policy Act

Regulation Status: Uruguay became the first Latin American country to sign the Council of Europe AI Convention (September 2025). Agesic released AI regulatory recommendations (July 2024). AI strategy under development.
Policy Lever: Agesic recommendations for public sector AI. Council of Europe Convention alignment. Data protection law provides foundation.
Governance Type: Agesic coordinates AI and data regulation. Internal data committees being created within government entities.
Actor Involvement: First Latin American CoE AI Convention signatory. Active in Santiago/Cartagena Declarations, OECD engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement. Agesic provides soft governance.
Specific Laws: Council of Europe AI Convention (signed Sep 2025)|Agesic AI Recommendations (July 2024)

Regulation Status: Uzbekistan's Senate approved comprehensive AI legislation on November 1, 2025, including human rights safeguards, deepfake labeling, and personal data protections. First Central Asian country to introduce AI regulation (2021). Strategy for AI Development until 2030.
Policy Lever: Comprehensive AI legislation with deepfake labeling and human rights safeguards. Strategy targeting $1.5B national AI market. Innovation regime for AI technologies since 2021.
Governance Type: Uzkomnazorat (State Inspectorate) has audit and sanction powers for AI systems. Ministry of Innovative Development coordinates.
Actor Involvement: CIS cooperation. Growing bilateral partnerships. First-mover advantage in Central Asian AI governance.
Enforcement Level: Uzkomnazorat has audit and sanction powers established by legislation.
Specific Laws: AI Legislation (Senate approved Nov 2025)|Strategy for AI Development until 2030|2021 AI Innovation Regime Resolution

Regulation Status: Vanuatu has no dedicated AI governance framework or regulation as of April 2026. The country remains in the early stages of digital transformation with no specific legislative attention to artificial intelligence.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments specifically targeting AI have been implemented. Vanuatu lacks dedicated AI policy mechanisms or regulatory tools.
Governance Type: There is no formal AI governance structure in Vanuatu. AI regulation and oversight are not institutionalized within any government body.
Actor Involvement: The Pacific Islands Forum represents Vanuatu's regional engagement on digital and emerging technology issues. International and regional bodies provide the primary avenue for AI-related policy discussion rather than domestic actors.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation given the absence of any AI-specific legal framework.

Regulation Status: The Vatican adopted binding AI Guidelines (January 2025) for Vatican City State and published the doctrinal document 'Antiqua et Nova' on AI ethics. The Rome Call for AI Ethics (2020) has been signed by multiple governments and tech companies.
Policy Lever: Binding internal AI guidelines for Vatican operations. Moral authority through Rome Call for AI Ethics. Doctrinal document 'Antiqua et Nova' provides theological framework.
Governance Type: Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development coordinates. Pontifical Academy for Life leads AI ethics research.
Actor Involvement: Unique moral authority role in international AI governance. Rome Call signed by Microsoft, IBM, FAO, and multiple governments. UNESCO cooperation.
Enforcement Level: Internal guidelines binding within Vatican City. No external enforcement authority.
Specific Laws: Vatican AI Guidelines (Jan 2025)|Antiqua et Nova (2025)|Rome Call for AI Ethics (2020)

Regulation Status: Venezuela has no dedicated AI legislation, regulatory framework, or national AI policy as of April 2026. The country's ongoing economic and political crisis continues to severely limit capacity for digital policy development and AI governance.
Policy Lever: No specific policy instruments, regulatory mechanisms, or governance tools for AI have been established. Venezuela lacks the institutional infrastructure and political will to implement AI-specific regulation.
Governance Type: There is no formal AI governance structure or designated regulatory body overseeing artificial intelligence in Venezuela. AI governance remains entirely absent from the country's institutional landscape.
Actor Involvement: Venezuela is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration (2023) representing minimal international AI engagement. Domestic actors have negligible involvement in AI policy development due to economic constraints and limited technical capacity.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, oversight bodies, or monitoring systems for AI regulation exist in Venezuela.

Regulation Status: Vietnam became the first ASEAN country to pass a binding AI law—Law No. 134/2025 (passed December 10, 2025, effective March 1, 2026)—with a four-tier risk classification, registration requirements, and fines up to VND 2 billion.
Policy Lever: Law 134/2025 with four-tier risk classification and mandatory registration for high-risk systems. Draft Law on Digital Technology also covering AI under consultation.
Governance Type: Ministry of Information and Communications coordinates implementation. Registration system for high-risk AI being established.
Actor Involvement: First ASEAN binding AI law. Active in ASEAN AI governance. Growing bilateral partnerships with Japan, South Korea.
Enforcement Level: Fines up to VND 2 billion under Law 134/2025. Enforcement mechanisms being operationalized following March 2026 entry into force.
Specific Laws: Law No. 134/2025 (effective March 2026)|Draft Law on Digital Technology

Regulation Status: Yemen has no AI governance. Ongoing conflict and humanitarian crisis preclude policy development.
Policy Lever: No instruments.
Governance Type: No governance.
Actor Involvement: No engagement.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement.

Regulation Status: Zambia lacks dedicated AI legislation as of April 2026, relying instead on indirect governance through existing cybersecurity and ICT frameworks. The National ICT Strategy references AI development, but no comprehensive AI-specific regulatory framework has been enacted. The country remains in early-stage engagement with AI policy development.
Policy Lever: AI governance operates indirectly through the Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act and the broader National ICT Strategy. ZICTA coordinates digital policy implementation, but lacks specific AI regulatory mechanisms or dedicated policy levers.
Governance Type: ZICTA (Zambia Information and Communications Technology Authority) serves as the primary digital governance body without a dedicated AI authority or ministry. Governance remains fragmented across multiple ICT-related agencies without centralized AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Zambia engages with the African Union and Smart Africa Alliance on digital policy, with EU partnership on digital innovation initiatives. International frameworks influence policy direction, but domestic AI governance structures remain underdeveloped.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated AI enforcement mechanisms exist; enforcement relies on general cybersecurity and ICT regulations with limited AI-specific oversight capacity.
Specific Laws: Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act

Regulation Status: Zimbabwe has articulated a risk-based approach framework for AI regulation aligned with its Institutional Strategic Plan, but no binding legislation has been enacted as of May 2026. The regulatory approach remains primarily aspirational, relying on non-binding guidelines and strategic frameworks without mandatory implementation mechanisms.
Policy Lever: Zimbabwe employs non-binding policy statements and strategic frameworks without enforceable legal instruments. The country relies on participation in African Union and SADC regional initiatives rather than domestic binding regulatory tools.
Governance Type: Zimbabwe lacks a dedicated AI governance body and relies on general regulatory coordination across existing agencies with dispersed institutional responsibility. Governance is characterized by ad hoc coordination without centralized AI authority or formal delegation structures.
Actor Involvement: Zimbabwe participates in African Union and SADC AI governance initiatives, providing some regional multi-stakeholder engagement. However, domestic actor involvement remains limited to government agencies with minimal structured input from industry, academia, or civil society.
Enforcement Level: No enforceable AI regulation exists, and aspirational frameworks lack sanctioning mechanisms or dedicated enforcement authority.
